subject: Chemistry
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi>NaCl</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi>BaSO</mi></msub><mn>4</mn></msub></math>, be the conductances (in S) measured for saturated aqueous solutions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>S</mi><msub><mi>O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> respectively, at a temperature T. Which of the following is false?
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi>NaCl</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi>BaSO</mi></msub><mn>4</mn></msub></math>, be the conductances (in S) measured for saturated aqueous solutions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>S</mi><msub><mi>O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> respectively, at a temperature T. Which of the following is false?
options: lonic mobilities of ions from both salts increase with T.
solution:
(i) Ionic mobilities decreases with increase in temperature due to increase in random motion and hence decrease in relaxation time so derease in drift speed.

(i) NaCl is completely soluble salt while BaSO4 is sparingly soluble salt so C1>>C2.

(ii) On increasse in temperature conductance increase.

subject: Chemistry
q: How many electrons would be required to deposit $6.35 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper at the cathode during the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of copper sulphate? (Atomic mass of copper $=63.5 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ Avogadro's constant):
question: How many electrons would be required to deposit $6.35 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper at the cathode during the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of copper sulphate? (Atomic mass of copper $=63.5 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ Avogadro's constant):
solution:
$\mathrm{Cu} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{++}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
i.e, to deposit 1 mole of $\mathrm{Cu}$ at cathode from $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ solution $=2$ moles of electrons are required
i.e, To deposit
$6.35 \mathrm{~g}=\frac{6.35}{63.5} \times 2=\frac{2}{10}=\frac{1}{5}$ moles
Thus total no. of electrons required $=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{5}$

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is electrolyzed between platinum electrode <math><mn>0.1</mn></math> Faraday electricity. How many mole of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> will be deposited at the cathode?
question: A solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is electrolyzed between platinum electrode <math><mn>0.1</mn></math> Faraday electricity. How many mole of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> will be deposited at the cathode?
solution:
Ni+2+2e-→Ni 
2 F  charge gives = 1 mole of Ni
0.1 F  charge gives =12×0.1=0.05  mole  Ni

subject: Chemistry
q: Which out of the following is a correct equation to show change in molar conductivity with respect to concentration for a weak electrolyte, if the symbols carry their usual meaning :
question: Which out of the following is a correct equation to show change in molar conductivity with respect to concentration for a weak electrolyte, if the symbols carry their usual meaning :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{HA}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{A}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\ & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\alpha^2 \mathrm{C}}{1-\alpha} \\ & \alpha^2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \alpha-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=0 \\ & \left(\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}}\right)^2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}}-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=0 \\ & \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \lambda_{\mathrm{m}} \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}-\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}\right)^2=0\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: When the sample of copper with zinc impurity is to be purified by electrolysis, the appropriate electrodes are
question: When the sample of copper with zinc impurity is to be purified by electrolysis, the appropriate electrodes are
options: cathode - pure zinc     anode - pure copper
cathode - impure sample     anode - pure copper
cathode - impure zinc     anode - impure sample
cathode - pure copper     anode - impure sample
solution: Pure metal always deposits at cathode

subject: Chemistry
q: The cell, $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0=1.10 \mathrm{~V}\right)$, was allowed to be completely discharged at $\mathrm{298~K}$. The relative concentration of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\left[\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right]$ is
question: The cell, $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0=1.10 \mathrm{~V}\right)$, was allowed to be completely discharged at $\mathrm{298~K}$. The relative concentration of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\left[\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right]$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \mathrm{Q} \\
& \text { Where } \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}
\end{aligned}$
For complete discharge $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0$
So $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right|=10^{37.3}$
Hence, (C) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following cell reaction Cd(s)+Hg2SO4( s)+95H2O(l)⇌CdSO4·95H2O(s)+2Hg(l).
The value of Ecell0 is 4.315 V at 25°C. If ΔH°=-825.2 kJ mol-1, the standard entropy change ΔS° in J K-1 is _________ . (Nearest integer)
[Given : Faraday constant =96487 C mol-1]
question:
Consider the following cell reaction Cd(s)+Hg2SO4( s)+95H2O(l)⇌CdSO4·95H2O(s)+2Hg(l).
The value of Ecell0 is 4.315 V at 25°C. If ΔH°=-825.2 kJ mol-1, the standard entropy change ΔS° in J K-1 is _________ . (Nearest integer)
[Given : Faraday constant =96487 C mol-1]
solution:
For given cell reaction n=2
and ΔG°=-nFE°,cell ΔG°=ΔH°-TΔS
Then ΔH°=825.2×103 J/mole
T=298 K
E°cell=4.315 V
F=96487 C
ΔS°=--nFE°cell-ΔHT
ΔS°=--2×96487×4.315--825.2×103298=832.682×103-825.2×103298=7482298×103
ΔS°=25.1 J/k

subject: Chemistry
q:
ECu2+|Cu0=+0.34 V
EZn2+|Zn0=−0.76 V
Identify the incorrect statement from the options below for the above cell:
question:
ECu2+|Cu0=+0.34 V
EZn2+|Zn0=−0.76 V
Identify the incorrect statement from the options below for the above cell:
options: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi>ext</mi></msub><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.1</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Zn</mi></math> dissolves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Zn</mi></math> electrode and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cu</mi></math> deposits at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cu</mi></math> electrode

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi>ext</mi></msub><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.1</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Zn</mi></math> dissolves at anode and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cu</mi></math> deposits at cathode

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi>ext</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.1</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> no flow of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>–</mo></msup></math> or current occurs
solution:
Ecell∘=ECu2+Cu∘−EZn2+Zn∘=1.1 V

So If E=1.1 V no electron will flow

At E>1.1 V cell act as electrolytic cell and electron will flow from Cu to Zn.

At E<1.1 V cell act as electrochemical so Zn dissolve and Cu deposit.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following two half-cell reactions along with the standard reduction potential given:
$CO_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow CH_3 OH + H_2 O \quad E°_{red} = 0.02$ V
$\dfrac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 O \quad E°_{red} = 1.23$ V
A fuel cell was set up using the above two reactions such that the cell operates under the standard condition of $1$ bar pressure and $298$ K temperature. The fuel cell works with $80\%$ efficiency. If the work derived from the cell using $1$ mol of $CH_3 OH$ is used to compress an ideal gas isothermally against a constant pressure of $1$ kPa, then the change in the volume of the gas, $\Delta V = $ _____ m$^3$. (nearest integer)
Given: $F = 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$
question:
Consider the following two half-cell reactions along with the standard reduction potential given:
$CO_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow CH_3 OH + H_2 O \quad E°_{red} = 0.02$ V
$\dfrac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 O \quad E°_{red} = 1.23$ V
A fuel cell was set up using the above two reactions such that the cell operates under the standard condition of $1$ bar pressure and $298$ K temperature. The fuel cell works with $80\%$ efficiency. If the work derived from the cell using $1$ mol of $CH_3 OH$ is used to compress an ideal gas isothermally against a constant pressure of $1$ kPa, then the change in the volume of the gas, $\Delta V = $ _____ m$^3$. (nearest integer)
Given: $F = 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$
solution:
The half-cell reactions for the fuel cell are:
Anode (Oxidation): $CH_3OH + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \quad E^{\circ}_{ox} = -0.02$ V
Cathode (Reduction): $\dfrac{3}{2}O_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 3H_2O \quad E^{\circ}_{red} = 1.23$ V

The overall cell reaction is:
$CH_3OH + \dfrac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$

The standard cell potential is:
$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = 1.23 - 0.02 = 1.21$ V

The number of electrons transferred per mole of $CH_3OH$ is $n = 6$.

The maximum electrical work that can be obtained from the cell is given by the decrease in Gibbs free energy:
$W_{max} = nFE^{\circ}_{cell} = 6 \times 96500 \times 1.21 = 700590$ J

Since the fuel cell operates at $80\%$ efficiency, the actual work derived is:
$W_{actual} = 0.80 \times 700590 = 560472$ J

This work is used to compress an ideal gas isothermally against a constant external pressure of $1$ kPa ($1000$ Pa). The work done on the gas during compression is positive:
$W = -P_{ext} \Delta V$

Substituting the values:
$560472 = -1000 \times \Delta V$
$\Delta V = -560.472$ m$^3$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the change in volume is $-560$ m$^3$.

Answer: $-560$

subject: Chemistry
q:
At what pH, given half cell MnO4-(0.1M)∣Mn2+ (0.001 M) will have electrode potential of 1.282 V ? (Nearest Integer)

Given EMnO4-/Mn2+o=1.54 V,2.303RTF=0.059 V
question:
At what pH, given half cell MnO4-(0.1M)∣Mn2+ (0.001 M) will have electrode potential of 1.282 V ? (Nearest Integer)

Given EMnO4-/Mn2+o=1.54 V,2.303RTF=0.059 V
solution:
MnO4-+8H++5e-⇌Mn2++4H2O

E=E°-0.0595logMn2+MnO4-H+8

1.282=1.54-0.0595log10-310-1×H+8

0.258×50.059=log10-2H+8

⇒21.86=-2+8pH

pH=2.98

≃3

subject: Chemistry
q: The molar conductance of an infinitely dilute solution of ammonium chloride was found to be $185 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the ionic conductance of hydroxyl and chloride ions are 170 and $70 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-}$ ${ }^1$, respectively. If molar conductance of 0.02 M solution of ammonium hydroxide is $85.5 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, its degree of dissociation is given by $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-1}$. The value of $x$ is ________ (Nearest integer)
question: The molar conductance of an infinitely dilute solution of ammonium chloride was found to be $185 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the ionic conductance of hydroxyl and chloride ions are 170 and $70 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-}$ ${ }^1$, respectively. If molar conductance of 0.02 M solution of ammonium hydroxide is $85.5 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, its degree of dissociation is given by $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-1}$. The value of $x$ is ________ (Nearest integer)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}=185 \\ & \left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}}+\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}=185 \\ & \left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}}=185-70=115 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}}=\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}}+\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{OH}^{-}} \\ & =115+170 \\ & \left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}}=285 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { degree of dissociation } & =\frac{\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}}}{\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\right)_{\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}}} \\ & =\frac{85.5}{285} \\ & =0.3 \\ & =3 \times 10^{-1}\end{aligned}$

Electrode potentials $\left(\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\right)$ are given below :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cu}^{+} / \mathrm{Cu}=+0.52 \mathrm{~V} ,\\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V}, \\
& \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s}) / \mathrm{I}^{-}=+0.54 \mathrm{~V}, \\
& \mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}=+0.88 \mathrm{~V}.
\end{aligned}$
Based on the above potentials, strongest oxidizing agent will be :
question:
Electrode potentials $\left(\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\right)$ are given below :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cu}^{+} / \mathrm{Cu}=+0.52 \mathrm{~V} ,\\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V}, \\
& \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s}) / \mathrm{I}^{-}=+0.54 \mathrm{~V}, \\
& \mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}=+0.88 \mathrm{~V}.
\end{aligned}$
Based on the above potentials, strongest oxidizing agent will be :
solution: Higher the value of reduction potential stronger will be the oxidising hence based on the given values $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$will be strongest oxidizing agent.

q:
The correct order of reduction potentials of the following pairs is

A. Cl2/Cl-

B. I2/I-

C. Ag+/Ag

D. Na+/Na

E. Li+/Li

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
The correct order of reduction potentials of the following pairs is

A. Cl2/Cl-

B. I2/I-

C. Ag+/Ag

D. Na+/Na

E. Li+/Li

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
ECl2∣Cl−0=1.36E∘I2∣I−=0.54ELi+|Li=−3.05E∘Ag+∣Ag=0.80ENa+|Na=−2.71

So, we can clearly see the order of reduction potential.

A>C>B>D>E

subject: Chemistry
q: The limiting molar conductivities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaI</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>NaNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mSm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively (all at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>). The limiting molar conductivity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgI</mi></math> at this temperature is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>mSm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The limiting molar conductivities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaI</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>NaNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mSm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively (all at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>). The limiting molar conductivity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgI</mi></math> at this temperature is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>mSm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
(1) λm∞NaI=12.7mSm2 mol-1

(2) λm∞NaNO3=12.0mSm2 mol-1

(3) λm∞AgNO3=13.3mSm2 mol-1

λm∞AgI=1+3-2

=12.7+13.3-12.0

=26.0-12.0

λm∞AgI=14.0

subject: Chemistry
q:
A metal surface of 100 cm2 area has to be coated with nickel layer of thickness 0.001 mm. A current of 2A was passed through a solution of NiNO32 for ‘x’ seconds to coat the desired layer. The value of x is _________. (Nearest integer)

(ρNi (density of Nickel) is 10 g mL–1, Molar mass of Nickel is 60 g mol-1 F=96500 Cmol-1)
question:
A metal surface of 100 cm2 area has to be coated with nickel layer of thickness 0.001 mm. A current of 2A was passed through a solution of NiNO32 for ‘x’ seconds to coat the desired layer. The value of x is _________. (Nearest integer)

(ρNi (density of Nickel) is 10 g mL–1, Molar mass of Nickel is 60 g mol-1 F=96500 Cmol-1)
solution:
One equivalent of substance deposited for 96500C(one Faraday).

i×t Columbus of charge can be utilised to get w g of substance.

Millimoles of Ni coated

=(2x)10096500×2

=10x965

Mass of Ni=x965600

=600x965mg

Volume=60x965×10-3 cm3

=100×10-4

⇒x=0.1608×103

≈161

subject: Chemistry
q: A variable, the opposite external potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi>ext</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math> is applied to the cell <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∥</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, of potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. When <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mn>1.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><mn>1.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, respectively electrons flow from

When <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mn>1.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><mn>1.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, respectively electrons flow from
options: anode to cathode in both the cases.
anode to cathode and cathode to anode.
cathode to anode and anode to cathode.
cathode to anode in both cases.
solution:
For a Daniel cell,

Ecell=0.34-(-0.76)=1.10 V.

When Eext<1.10 V, electrons flow from anode to cathode in the external circuit.

When Eext>1.10 V, electrons flow from cathode to anode in the external circuit (reverse reaction).

subject: Chemistry
q:
MX is a sparingly soluble salt that follows the given solubility equilibrium at 298 K.
$\mathrm{MX}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{M}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{X}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=10^{-10}$
If the standard reduction potential for $\mathrm{M}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow{+\mathrm{e}^{-}} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{s})$ is $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}\right)=0.79 \mathrm{~V}$, then the value of the standard reduction potential for the metal/metal insoluble salt electrode $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}^{-} / \mathrm{MX}(\mathrm{s}) / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ mV. (nearest integer)
[Given : $\frac{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}=0.059 \mathrm{~V}$ ]
question:
MX is a sparingly soluble salt that follows the given solubility equilibrium at 298 K.
$\mathrm{MX}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{M}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{X}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=10^{-10}$
If the standard reduction potential for $\mathrm{M}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow{+\mathrm{e}^{-}} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{s})$ is $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}\right)=0.79 \mathrm{~V}$, then the value of the standard reduction potential for the metal/metal insoluble salt electrode $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}^{-} / \mathrm{MX}(\mathrm{s}) / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ mV. (nearest integer)
[Given : $\frac{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}=0.059 \mathrm{~V}$ ]
solution:
The standard reduction potential for a metal/metal insoluble salt electrode is related to the standard reduction potential of the metal ion and the solubility product $K_{sp}$ of the salt.
The half-cell reaction for the $X^-/MX(s)/M$ electrode is: $MX(s) + e^- \rightarrow M(s) + X^-(aq)$.
This can be viewed as a combination of two processes:
1. $MX(s) \rightleftharpoons M^+(aq) + X^-(aq)$ with equilibrium constant $K_{sp} = 10^{-10}$
2. $M^+(aq) + e^- \rightarrow M(s)$ with $E^\ominus_{M^+/M} = 0.79\text{ V}$
The relation between the standard potentials is given by:
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = E^\ominus_{M^+/M} + \frac{2.303RT}{nF} \log_{10} K_{sp}$
Given $n = 1$ and $\frac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.059\text{ V}$:
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = 0.79 + 0.059 \log_{10}(10^{-10})$
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = 0.79 + 0.059(-10)$
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = 0.79 - 0.59 = 0.20\text{ V}$
To convert the value into mV:
$0.20\text{ V} = 0.20 \times 1000\text{ mV} = 200\text{ mV}$

subject: Chemistry
q: In an electrochemical reaction of lead, at standard temperature, if  EPb2+/Pbo=m Volt  and  EPb4+/Pbo=n Volt,then the value of  E°Pb2+/Pb4+  is given by m – xn. The value of x is _______. (Nearest integer)
question: In an electrochemical reaction of lead, at standard temperature, if  EPb2+/Pbo=m Volt  and  EPb4+/Pbo=n Volt,then the value of  E°Pb2+/Pb4+  is given by m – xn. The value of x is _______. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The relation between Gibbs Free energy and emf of the cell can be related as follows,

∆G=-nFEcell

The electrode reactions can be written as follows,

Pb2++2e-→Pb       ∆G1=-2FEPb2+/Pbo

Pb4++4e-→Pb       ∆G2=-4FEPb4+/Pbo

Pb4++2e-→Pb2+       ∆G3=-2FEPb4+/Pb2+o

Now, ∆G3=∆G2-∆G1

-2FEPb4+/Pb2+o=-4FEPb4+/Pbo+2FEPb2+/Pbo

⇒4EPb+4/Pbo=2EPb+2/Pbo+2EPb+4/Pb+2o

⇒4n=2m+2EPb+4/Pb+2o⇒EPb+4/Pb+2o=2n-m⇒EPb+2/Pb+4o=m-2n

Hence, the value of x=2

subject: Chemistry
q:
For a cell, CusCu2+0.001M)Ag+0.01MAgsthe cell potential is found to be 0.43 V at 298 K. The magnitude of standard electrode potential for Cu2+|Cu is____×10-2 V

Given: EAg+/AgΘ=0.80 V and 2.303RTF=0.06 V
question:
For a cell, CusCu2+0.001M)Ag+0.01MAgsthe cell potential is found to be 0.43 V at 298 K. The magnitude of standard electrode potential for Cu2+|Cu is____×10-2 V

Given: EAg+/AgΘ=0.80 V and 2.303RTF=0.06 V
solution:
At anode Cu→Cu2++2e-

At cathode 2Ag++2e-→2Ag

Cell reaction →Cu+2Ag+→Cu2++2Ag

Ecell=Ecell0-0.062logCu2+Ag+2

0.43=Ecell0-0.062log0.0010.012

Ecell0=0.46

Ecell0=EAg+/Ag0-ECu2+/Cu0

0.46=0.80-ECu2+/Cu0

ECu2+/Cu0=0.34 volt

ECu2+/Cu0=34×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The limiting molar conductivity of KCl (strong electrolyte) is higher compared to that of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (weak electrolyte).
Statement II : Molar conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration of electrolyte.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The limiting molar conductivity of KCl (strong electrolyte) is higher compared to that of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (weak electrolyte).
Statement II : Molar conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration of electrolyte.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
lon K+ Cl- CH3COO- H+
λ∘/ S cm2 mol-1 73.5 76.3 40.9 349.6

λ∘MCH3COOH=λ∘MH++λ∘MCH3COO−

=349.6+40.9=390.5Scm2mol−1

λ∘M(KCl)=λ∘MK++λ∘MCl−

=73.5+76.3=149.8Scm2mol−1

So, statement-I is false.
For an electrolyte the dilution increases (or) decrease in concentration, the number of ions increases due to increase of degree of dissociation. So, the molar conductance increases. Hence, Statement-II is false.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The cell potential for the following cell PtH2gH+aq||Cu2+0.01MCus is 0.576 V at 298 K. The pH of the solution is (Nearest integer)

(Given : ECu2+/Cuo=0.34 V and 2.303RTF=0.06 V)
question:
The cell potential for the following cell PtH2gH+aq||Cu2+0.01MCus is 0.576 V at 298 K. The pH of the solution is (Nearest integer)

(Given : ECu2+/Cuo=0.34 V and 2.303RTF=0.06 V)
solution:
Anode ⇒H2g⟶2H+aq+2e-

Cathode ⇒Cu2+aq+2e-⟶Cus

Overall ⇒H2q+Cu2+aq⟶2H+aq+Cus¯

Ecell°=ECu2+Cu°-EH+H2°=0.34 V

Ecell=Ecell°-0.062logH+2Cu2+

0.576=0.34+0.03-logH+2+logCu2+

0.576=0.34+0.032pH+logCu2+

0.236=0.032pH-2

7.866=2pH-2

pH=4.93≈5

subject: Chemistry
q: The mass of silver (Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ag</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>108</mn><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) displaced by a quantity of electricity which displaces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at S.T.P. will be _____ g.
question: The mass of silver (Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ag</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>108</mn><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) displaced by a quantity of electricity which displaces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at S.T.P. will be _____ g.
solution:
Given Molar mass of Ag:108 gmol-1

Equal equivalents of substances deposited or liberated when some quantity of electricity passed through circuite in the electrolysis cell.

Equivalents of silver deposited= Equivalents of  O2 liberated.
Let x gm silver displaced,
(Molar volume of gas at STP =22.4L )

Number equivalents of a substance = The number of moles of substance × Valence factor of that substance
x×1108=5.622.4×4,x=108 gm.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reaction

MnO4-+8H++5e-→Mn+2+4H2O ,E°=1.51 V

The quantity of electricity required in Faraday to reduce five moles of MnO4- is
question:
Consider the following reaction

MnO4-+8H++5e-→Mn+2+4H2O ,E°=1.51 V

The quantity of electricity required in Faraday to reduce five moles of MnO4- is
solution:
For this balanced reaction, the change in oxidation number of the metal ion is Mn+7 →Mn+2.

Therefore one mole Mn required 5 Faraday

Hence, for five mole Mn required 25 Faraday.

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the given reactions

Sn2++2e-→Sn

Sn4++4e-→Sn the electrode potentials are ; ESn2+/Sn°=-0.140 V and ESn4+/Sn°=0.010 V. The magnitude of standard electrode potential for Sn4+/Sn2+ i.e. ESn4+/Sn2+° is____×10-2 V(Nearest integer)
question:
For the given reactions

Sn2++2e-→Sn

Sn4++4e-→Sn the electrode potentials are ; ESn2+/Sn°=-0.140 V and ESn4+/Sn°=0.010 V. The magnitude of standard electrode potential for Sn4+/Sn2+ i.e. ESn4+/Sn2+° is____×10-2 V(Nearest integer)
solution:
(i) Sn2++2e-⟶Sn         E10=-0.14 V

ΔG10=-2 F-0.14

(ii) Sn4++4e-⟶Sn       E20=+0.010 VΔG20=-4F+0.010

Target Sn4++2e-⟶Sn2+      E30=?

ΔG30=-2 FE30

Target Eq.=Eq. ii - Eq. i

-2FE30=-4F0.010--2F-0.14

E30=4×0.010+2×0.142=0.16 V=16×10-2 V

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List-I with List-II.

  List-I   List-II
A Cds+2NiOH3s→CdOs+2NiOH2s+H2Ol I Primary battery
B ZnHg+HgOs→ZnOs+Hgl II Discharging of secondary battery
C 2PbSO4s+2H2OI→Pbs+PbO2s+2H2SO4aq III Fuel cell
D 2H2g+O2g→2H2Ol IV Charging of secondary battery

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Match List-I with List-II.

  List-I   List-II
A Cds+2NiOH3s→CdOs+2NiOH2s+H2Ol I Primary battery
B ZnHg+HgOs→ZnOs+Hgl II Discharging of secondary battery
C 2PbSO4s+2H2OI→Pbs+PbO2s+2H2SO4aq III Fuel cell
D 2H2g+O2g→2H2Ol IV Charging of secondary battery

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
(a)Cds+2NiOH3s→CdOs+2NiOH2s+H2Ol 
Discharge of secondary Battery

(b)ZnHg+HgOs→ZnOs+Hgl 
(Primary Battery Mercury cell)

(c) 2PbSO4s+2H2Ol→Pbs+PbO2s+2H2SO4aq Charging of secondary Battery

(d) 2H2g+O2g→2H2Ol- Fuel cell

Mercury cell, suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, watches, etc. consists of zinc - mercury amalgam as anode and a paste of HgO and carbon as the cathode. The electrolyte is a paste of KOH and ZnO. The electrode reactions for the cell are given below:

Anode: ZnHg+2OH-→ZnOs+H2O+2e-

Cathode: HgO+H2O+2e-→Hgl+2OH-

Another important secondary cell is the nickel-cadmium cell which has longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive to manufacture.

The overall reaction during discharge is:

Cds+2NiOH3s→CdOs+2NiOH2s+H2Ol

Fuel cell using H2 and O2 produces electricity.

Cathode: O2g+2H2Ol+4e-→4OH-aq

Anode: 2H2g+4OH-aq→4H2Ol+4e-

Overall reaction being:

2H2g+O2g→2H2Ol

(ii) Cell reaction of lead storage battery during recharge -

The reverse of oxidation at anode during discharge is the reduction reaction at cathode during recharging.

Pbaq2++2e-→Pbs

PbSO4s→Pbaq2++SO4aq2-

Final Reaction: PbSO4s+2e-→Pbs+SO4aq2-

q: The resistance of conductivity cell with cell constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>KCl</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1500</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The molar conductivity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>KCl</mi></math> solution at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is (Integer answer)
question: The resistance of conductivity cell with cell constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>KCl</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1500</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The molar conductivity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>KCl</mi></math> solution at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is (Integer answer)
solution:
k=1R×ℓA=11500×1.14 S cm-1

⇒∧m=1000×1.1415000.001 S cm2 mol-1

=760 S cm2 mol-1⇒760

subject: Chemistry
q:
The potential for the given half cell at 298K is -............ ×10-2V.

2H(aq)++2e-→H2( g)

H+=1M,PH2=2 atm

(Given2.303 RT/F=0.06 V, log2=0.3)
question:
The potential for the given half cell at 298K is -............ ×10-2V.

2H(aq)++2e-→H2( g)

H+=1M,PH2=2 atm

(Given2.303 RT/F=0.06 V, log2=0.3)
solution:
The Nernst equation for the given cell reaction 2H++2e-→H2 is 

E=E°-0.062×log pH2H+2

E=0-0.062log212

E=-0.03×0.3=-0.009=-9×10-3

E=-0.9×10-2V

subject: Chemistry
q: When 9.65 ampere current was passed for 1.0 hour into nitrobenzene in acidic medium, the amount of p-aminophenol produced is:
question: When 9.65 ampere current was passed for 1.0 hour into nitrobenzene in acidic medium, the amount of p-aminophenol produced is:
solution:
4e-+4H++C6H5NO2 → C6H4OHNH2+H2O

Molecular Weight of  p-aminophenol =  109 g

W (Deposited)   = ZIt

=EF ×I ×t             E=M4

W=109 ×9.65 ×60 ×604 ×96500=9.81 g

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following ions does not liberate hydrogen gas on reaction with dilute acids?
question: Which of the following ions does not liberate hydrogen gas on reaction with dilute acids?
solution:
Mn2+ does not displace H2 from dilute acid because they are less reactive SRP value. 

Given below are SRP values,
H2O|H2=-0.8277, Mn2+|Mn=-1.185 V
